statement: Every page or piece of content must be reachable within three clicks or users will give up.
why_it_spreads: It is a memorable, quantifiable rule of thumb that surfaced in late-1990s web-design folklore and Jeffrey Zeldman's 2001 book, and it gives teams a simple target.
evidence_against: NN/g (src-nngroup, https://www.nngroup.com/articles/3-click-rule/): no published study supports it; Porter's study found drop-off and satisfaction did not worsen past three clicks.
What matters is interaction cost and wayfinding clarity, not a raw click count.
replacement_principle: Minimise total interaction and cognitive cost and keep each step clear and confidence-building, rather than capping the number of clicks.
still_applies_when: As a loose heuristic to question unnecessarily deep navigation
When each extra step genuinely adds friction with no orientation benefit
